message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead">
You are the transcript-agent on team "phase3-detail". Your task is to implement the fullscreen transcript view for the mementor TUI app.

Read task #1 from the task list for full requirements. Then:

1. Read the existing code to understand the codebase:
   - `crates/mementor-tui/src/views/dashboard.rs` (example view pattern)
   - `crates/mementor-tui/src/app.rs` (App struct for context)
   - `crates/mementor-tui/Cargo.toml` (available deps)
   - `crates/mementor-lib/src/model/transcript.rs` (data types)
   - `crates/mementor-lib/src/entire/transcript.rs` (parsing, for understanding data)

2. Create `crates/mementor-tui/src/views/transcript.rs` with:
   - `TranscriptViewState` struct with scroll, expanded tools, sidebar toggle, search state
   - `TranscriptAction` enum: None, Back, ScrollTo(usize)
   - `render()` function: two-panel (transcript + tool sidebar), message rendering by type
   - `handle_key()` function: scrolling, search, tool expand/collapse, sidebar toggle
   - Message rendering: User (green), Assistant (blue), Thinking (dim gray collapsible), ToolUse (bordered box), ToolResult (under tool)
   - Search with `/` input mode, `n`/`N` cycling, yellow highlight on matches
   - Tool index sidebar on right 25%

3. Do NOT modify any other files (app.rs, views/mod.rs, etc.) — the lead handles integration.

4. After writing the file, run:
   - Add `pub mod transcript;` to `views/mod.rs` temporarily so it compiles
   - `cargo clippy -p mementor-tui -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --check -p mementor-tui`
   - Fix any issues
   - Then REVERT the views/mod.rs change (remove the `pub mod transcript;` line)

5. Mark task #1 as completed when done.

IMPORTANT: Use Rust edition 2024 features (let chains in if-let). The crate uses `edition = "2024"` and `resolver = "3"`.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT use `unicode-width` crate — it's not in the dependencies.
</teammate-message>

content.text: I'll start by reading the task details and understanding the existing codebase.

content.content:
Task #1: Implement fullscreen transcript view
Status: pending
Description: Create `crates/mementor-tui/src/views/transcript.rs` with a self-contained fullscreen transcript viewer.

**State struct**: `TranscriptViewState` with:
- `scroll_offset: usize` — current scroll position (line-based)
- `expanded_tools: HashSet<usize>` — indices of expanded tool call blocks
- `show_sidebar: bool` — tool index sidebar visibility (default true)
- `search_query: Option<String>` — active search text
- `search_matches: Vec<usize>` — line indices matching search
- `search_index: usize` — current match index for n/N cycling
- `search_input_active: bool` — whether `/` search input mode is active

**Rendering**:
- Two-panel layout when sidebar visible: left 75% transcript, right 25% tool index
- Single-panel (full width) when sidebar hidden
- Message rendering by type:
  - User: Green "[User]" header with timestamp, plain text body
  - Assistant: Blue "[Assistant]" header, plain text body
  - Thinking: Dim gray italic, collapsible (show first line + "..." when collapsed)
  - ToolUse: Bordered box with tool name header, args preview (truncated), result preview when expanded
  - ToolResult: Indented under tool use, truncated with "..." (shown only when parent tool is expanded)
- Tool index sidebar: list of tool names with index numbers, highlight current
- Search highlights: Yellow background on matching text

**Key bindings**:
- `j`/`k`/`↑`/`↓`: Scroll line by line
- `Ctrl-d`/`Ctrl-u`: Scroll half page
- `g`/`G`: Jump to top/bottom
- `Enter`: Expand/collapse tool call at current scroll position
- `/`: Enter search mode (show input at bottom)
- `n`/`N`: Next/prev search match
- `o`: Toggle tool index sidebar
- `Esc`: Return action (back to previous view) — but do NOT wire this into App, just return an action enum

**Action enum**: `TranscriptAction` with variants: `None`, `Back`, `ScrollTo(usize)`

**Function signatures**:
- `pub fn render(frame: &mut Frame, area: Rect, state: &mut TranscriptViewState, entries: &[TranscriptEntry])`
- `pub fn handle_key(key: KeyEvent, state: &mut TranscriptViewState, entry_count: usize) -> TranscriptAction`

The render function takes `entries: &[TranscriptEntry]` from mementor_lib::model. Import types from `mementor_lib::model::{TranscriptEntry, TranscriptMessage, ContentBlock, MessageRole}`.

**Important**: This module should be self-contained. Do NOT modify app.rs or any other files. The lead will integrate this into the app.

Available dependencies in mementor-tui's Cargo.toml: ratatui, crossterm, mementor-lib, jiff, anyhow, tokio, futures, tracing.

Must pass `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and `cargo fmt --check`. Use Rust edition 2024 features (let chains in if-let, etc.).
Blocks: #3

task.subject: Implement fullscreen transcript view
task.description:
Create `crates/mementor-tui/src/views/transcript.rs` with a self-contained fullscreen transcript viewer.

**State struct**: `TranscriptViewState` with:
- `scroll_offset: usize` — current scroll position (line-based)
- `expanded_tools: HashSet<usize>` — indices of expanded tool call blocks
- `show_sidebar: bool` — tool index sidebar visibility (default true)
- `search_query: Option<String>` — active search text
- `search_matches: Vec<usize>` — line indices matching search
- `search_index: usize` — current match index for n/N cycling
- `search_input_active: bool` — whether `/` search input mode is active

**Rendering**:
- Two-panel layout when sidebar visible: left 75% transcript, right 25% tool index
- Single-panel (full width) when sidebar hidden
- Message rendering by type:
  - User: Green "[User]" header with timestamp, plain text body
  - Assistant: Blue "[Assistant]" header, plain text body
  - Thinking: Dim gray italic, collapsible (show first line + "..." when collapsed)
  - ToolUse: Bordered box with tool name header, args preview (truncated), result preview when expanded
  - ToolResult: Indented under tool use, truncated with "..." (shown only when parent tool is expanded)
- Tool index sidebar: list of tool names with index numbers, highlight current
- Search highlights: Yellow background on matching text

**Key bindings**:
- `j`/`k`/`↑`/`↓`: Scroll line by line
- `Ctrl-d`/`Ctrl-u`: Scroll half page
- `g`/`G`: Jump to top/bottom
- `Enter`: Expand/collapse tool call at current scroll position
- `/`: Enter search mode (show input at bottom)
- `n`/`N`: Next/prev search match
- `o`: Toggle tool index sidebar
- `Esc`: Return action (back to previous view) — but do NOT wire this into App, just return an action enum

**Action enum**: `TranscriptAction` with variants: `None`, `Back`, `ScrollTo(usize)`

**Function signatures**:
- `pub fn render(frame: &mut Frame, area: Rect, state: &mut TranscriptViewState, entries: &[TranscriptEntry])`
- `pub fn handle_key(key: KeyEvent, state: &mut TranscriptViewState, entry_count: usize) -> TranscriptAction`

The render function takes `entries: &[TranscriptEntry]` from mementor_lib::model. Import types from `mementor_lib::model::{TranscriptEntry, TranscriptMessage, ContentBlock, MessageRole}`.

**Important**: This module should be self-contained. Do NOT modify app.rs or any other files. The lead will integrate this into the app.

Available dependencies in mementor-tui's Cargo.toml: ratatui, crossterm, mementor-lib, jiff, anyhow, tokio, futures, tracing.

Must pass `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and `cargo fmt --check`. Use Rust edition 2024 features (let chains in if-let, etc.).

file.content:
[package]
name = "mementor-tui"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[lints]
workspace = true

[dependencies]
mementor-lib = { path = "../mementor-lib" }
ratatui = { version = "0.29", default-features = false, features = ["crossterm"] }
crossterm = "0.28"
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt-multi-thread", "macros"] }
anyhow = "1"
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter"] }
futures = "0.3.32"
jiff = "0.2.21"

content.content:
1→/// Role of a message in a transcript.
     2→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
     3→pub enum MessageRole {
     4→    User,
     5→    Assistant,
     6→}
     7→
     8→/// A content block within an assistant message.
     9→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    10→pub enum ContentBlock {
    11→    Text(String),
    12→    Thinking(String),
    13→    ToolUse {
    14→        name: String,
    15→        input: serde_json::Value,
    16→    },
    17→    ToolResult {
    18→        tool_use_id: String,
    19→        content: String,
    20→    },
    21→}
    22→
    23→/// A single message in a transcript.
    24→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    25→pub struct TranscriptMessage {
    26→    pub role: MessageRole,
    27→    pub uuid: String,
    28→    pub timestamp: Option<String>,
    29→    pub content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
    30→}
    31→
    32→/// An entry in a JSONL transcript file.
    33→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    34→pub enum TranscriptEntry {
    35→    Message(TranscriptMessage),
    36→    FileHistorySnapshot {
    37→        files: Vec<String>,
    38→    },
    39→    PrLink {
    40→        pr_number: u64,
    41→        pr_url: String,
    42→        repository: String,
    43→    },
    44→    Progress(String),
    45→    Other(String),
    46→}
    47→
    48→/// A group of transcript entries for display in the TUI.
    49→///
    50→/// Typically represents a user message + assistant response pair.
    51→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    52→pub struct ConversationSegment {
    53→    pub entries: Vec<TranscriptEntry>,
    54→}
    55→

content.thinking: Now let me also read the views/mod.rs to understand the module structure, and check what other views look like.

content.text: Now let me read the views module structure and check for any other relevant files.

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me mark task #1 as in_progress and start implementing the transcript view.

Key things to note:
1. The view pattern: functions `render()` and `handle_key()` are standalone functions, not methods on a struct
2. The transcript view takes `entries: &[TranscriptEntry]` directly, not `&mut App`
3. It should be self-contained with its own state struct
4. Edition 2024 features: let chains in if-let
5. Available ratatui version 0.29, crossterm 0.28

Let me design the implementation:


I need to define the state struct with scroll tracking, expandable tool call sections, a sidebar toggle, and search functionality including an active search input buffer, match indices, and navigation through results.

For actions, I'll handle navigation back, scrolling to specific positions, and search interactions.

The rendering splits the viewport into a transcript panel on the left and a collapsible tool sidebar on the right, with each message type styled distinctly—user messages in green, assistant in blue, thinking blocks collapsible and dimmed, tool calls in bordered boxes with truncated previews, and results indented underneath. Search is triggered with `/` and filters lines as the user types.

I'm thinking through the rendering strategy more carefully. Since transcripts can get long, I'll pre-compute all the renderable lines as `Line` objects, then apply scroll offsets to show only what fits in the viewport and highlight any search matches in yellow. For tracking expanded tool calls, I need to maintain state on which ones are open.

Defining the state structure... Building helper functions for rendering...

Now I need to handle the tool use and tool result pairing—each ToolUse gets a sequential index, and I'll match ToolResults to their parent tools by tracking which tool use each result belongs to, since they appear in order within the message's content blocks.

My approach is to do a first pass collecting all lines with their associated tool indices, then apply search highlighting if needed, and finally render only the visible portion based on scroll offset.

Writing the Rust implementation... Initializing the default state with all fields set to their empty or zero values, then implementing the Default trait to delegate to the new constructor. Now I'm thinking about how to render the tool calls with bordered boxes in the transcript view—ratatui makes it tricky to draw borders inline within scrolling text, so I'm considering using Unicode box-drawing characters to simulate borders instead of relying on actual UI components.

I'm sketching out two visual styles: a collapsed view showing just the tool name with a right-pointing arrow and a preview of the arguments, and an expanded view that displays the full arguments and result sections with vertical lines and separators to create the box effect.

Now I need to handle search highlighting by applying a yellow background to matching text spans.

For the implementation, I'm structuring this as a vector of renderable lines paired with a parallel vector tracking which tool each line belongs to, then building a sidebar with the tool list. The key question is how the tool indexing works with the expanded tools set—I'm thinking the index refers to the position in the tools vector.

When a user selects a line that maps to a tool, toggling that tool's expanded state will control whether its content displays. I also need to handle thinking blocks as collapsible, dimmed sections.

The task specifies `expanded_tools: HashSet<usize>` for tracking expanded tool blocks, but thinking blocks need the same collapsible behavior. I could either use a separate `expanded_thinking` set or reuse the same set with different index ranges for both block types. Since the spec is explicit about the field name, I'll stick with `expanded_tools` and treat it as a general collapsible block index that covers both tools and thinking sections. I'm settling on a single sequential index for all collapsible blocks—both thinking and tool use—tracked in the same `expanded_tools` set, with Enter toggling whichever block is at the current scroll position. Now I'll start implementing the content builder that iterates through entries and constructs the lines while managing these indices.

Building the content renderer... Building the message rendering logic... Writing the thinking block renderer... Building the tool sidebar display... Building the rendering logic...

I'm realizing the pairing between ToolUse and ToolResult blocks needs better tracking—when I encounter a ToolResult, I need to know which ToolUse it belongs to so I can nest it properly under the parent tool when expanded. I should track the last ToolUse's index as I iterate through the content blocks, then use that to establish the parent-child relationship for display purposes. I need to track the last ToolUse's block_index so I can reference it when a ToolResult appears. The tricky part is handling borders correctly—if a tool is expanded, I shouldn't render the ToolUse's bottom border until after the ToolResult is rendered. So I'll process blocks linearly: render ToolUse with its top border and args, skip the bottom border if expanded, then when ToolResult comes through, render it only if the parent tool is expanded, followed by the bottom border.

Actually, I think the cleanest approach is to preprocess the content blocks into grouped units—pairing each ToolUse with its matching ToolResult if one exists. Then I can render each complete tool block as a single unit.

Defining a processed block enum...

Now I'm thinking through the rendering logic for collapsed versus expanded tools. When a tool is collapsed, I show just the name and a preview of the arguments. When expanded, I display the full arguments and the result section below. Let me write out the complete implementation with this structure in mind.

I should also consider how search highlighting will work across these tool blocks.

Writing the complete implementation... I need to figure out whether I can actually use `serde_json::Value` directly in my code without importing serde_json as a dependency, since mementor-lib exposes it in its public API but mementor-tui doesn't depend on serde_json directly—I can reference the type through the type signature, but calling methods on it might require the crate to be in scope. Value type implements `Display` through the `serde_json` crate, so calling `.to_string()` on it will work fine for the args preview—I don't need to worry about pretty printing for now.

For rendering the transcript, I'm thinking about using `Paragraph::scroll()` to handle potentially long entries by only displaying the visible portion. It's the simplest approach—build all the lines, create a Paragraph, and offset with `.scroll()`. There's a theoretical limit of 65535 lines since it takes `u16` values, but that's not a practical concern for now.

Now I'm writing out the full implementation with the necessary imports and structure.

I need to think through the line building more carefully. I want a function that converts transcript entries into Lines, then another that applies search highlighting by splitting spans when matches are found and coloring the matched portions with a yellow background.

Writing the highlight function... Finishing the span construction...

I'm realizing there's a potential issue with byte position mismatches when converting to lowercase for Unicode characters, though for ASCII this approach works fine. I should probably stick with ASCII case-insensitivity to keep things simple and avoid complications with character encoding.

Actually, let me just go with case-sensitive matching instead—it's simpler, more predictable, and most code search is case-sensitive anyway.

Wait, but the task doesn't specify, so case-insensitive would probably give better UX. I'll stick with that approach using lowercased strings, accepting that non-ASCII edge cases might cause slight highlighting misalignment.

Hold on, I'm overcomplicating this. The search_matches are line indices, not character positions. I just need to find which lines contain the query, then do the highlighting within those matched lines. The cycling through matches (n/N) moves between line indices.

Let me start implementing the full solution now.

Now I'm thinking through how to render ToolUse blocks with borders...

Defining a structure to represent rendered blocks...

Now I need to map each line in the output to its corresponding block so I can track which tool got expanded or collapsed. When the user presses Enter at the current scroll position, I'll look up that line in the map to find which block to toggle. The scroll offset represents the top line of the visible area, so I need to make sure the mapping stays perfectly aligned with the rendered lines. I need to add a tool sidebar on the right that displays all tools with their index numbers, highlighting whichever tool contains the current scroll position. I also need to handle the search input UI—when it's active, there should be a search bar at the bottom of the transcript area that takes up one line, reducing the available space for the transcript.

Let me sketch out the overall layout: the left side has the scrollable transcript content with the search input bar at the bottom when active, and the right side has a fixed tools sidebar. I need to be careful about line counting when calculating scroll bounds, since the search input affects how many lines are available for the transcript.

Writing the layout code... Writing the complete implementation...

I'm working through the `serde_json` dependency issue—since it's not a direct dependency of `mementor-tui`, I can't rely on `serde_json::to_string_pretty()` even though the type appears in the public API. I'll need to either manually format the JSON with indentation or stick with compact formatting using the `Display` trait. I can use types from transitive dependencies if they're in a public API, but I can't directly call functions from those crates. So `value.to_string()` works because `Display` travels with the type, but `serde_json::to_string_pretty()` won't work since `serde_json` isn't explicitly declared. I'll stick with `to_string()` for the compact view and implement a simple manual indentation approach for the expanded version instead. I'm writing a helper function to format the args for the expanded view, iterating through the JSON object's key-value pairs and displaying each on its own line with indentation.

Wait, I realize I can actually use `serde_json::Value` directly since it's already part of the `ContentBlock::ToolUse` definition in `mementor_lib`, so the compiler will know the type when I pattern match on it and call methods like `as_object()` or `to_string()`.

The real constraint is if I need to write a function signature that explicitly names `serde_json::Value` as a parameter type—that would require adding `serde_json` to my direct dependencies, since transitive dependencies aren't automatically available as crate names in Rust.

To sidestep this entirely, I'll just handle the formatting inline within the match expression rather than extracting it into separate helper functions that would need explicit type annotations.

Writing the module code... Defining the TranscriptAction enum to represent different outcomes from key handling, then implementing the constructor for TranscriptViewState to initialize all fields with their default values. Building the layout structure... Finishing the search highlighting and rendering the transcript with scroll support, then building out the search bar UI to show match counts and query feedback. Handling key input for the transcript view, starting with search mode detection, then processing navigation commands like scrolling up and down with both vim-style and arrow keys, clamping the scroll offset to valid bounds.

Still writing input handlers... Writing keyboard handlers... Writing the search handler... Building the content renderer... Finishing up the pattern matching for different transcript entry types, handling PR links with styled formatting and progress indicators, then closing out the function by adding blank lines between entries and returning the collected lines, tool mappings, and sidebar data.

Now I'm setting up the render_message function to format transcript messages with role-based styling...

Formatting the message header with timestamp...

Processing content blocks...

Handling text and thinking blocks... Now I'm handling the tool block case, where I need to check if it's expanded and set up the styling, then record it in the sidebar and start drawing the border with the tool name.

Building the tool display... Still writing the display logic...

Actually, I'm realizing there's an issue with how I structured the `ProcessedBlock` enum. The current design tries to store a `block_index` field in the `Thinking` variant, but that doesn't match the actual struct definition. I need to simplify this—the enum should just hold the block data itself (text, thinking content, or tool information), and track the block index separately as I iterate through them.

Let me refactor the pattern matching in `render_message` to work with this cleaner design, where I extract the thinking text directly and manage the index externally. Then I'll move on to implementing `preprocess_content_blocks`, which will iterate through the content blocks and convert them into my `ProcessedBlock` variants.

Defining the preprocessing function... I'm handling orphaned tool results by displaying them as text, then moving on to render the sidebar. Building the sidebar layout... Building the sidebar list...

Now I'm realizing the current tool detection needs to account for the end_line of each entry too, not just the start_line, so I can properly determine which tool's range contains the current scroll offset.

I could use the tool_line_map to look up which block_index corresponds to the scroll position, then match that back to a sidebar entry. To make this work, I'll add a block_index field to ToolSidebarEntry so there's a direct mapping between the sidebar and the block indices tracked in tool_line_map.

Refining the sidebar rendering function... Finishing the list widget setup and now thinking through how to compute search matches—I should calculate them in the render function after building the lines, filtering for any that contain the search query text.

Still writing search logic... Finishing the highlight logic...

Actually, I'm realizing there's a logic issue here. If no matches are found, the loop never runs, so `last` stays at 0, and then the `if last < content.len()` condition adds the entire span with its original style. That handles the no-match case correctly, so the final check for `last == 0` is redundant—I can remove it.

But I need to be careful with empty content. If the span is empty, `content.len()` is 0, so neither the loop nor the final `if` would execute, leaving me with an empty spans vector. Let me refactor to handle that edge case properly while extracting the style from each span. Writing helper functions... Finishing the timestamp formatting logic...

Now I'm reviewing the code for potential issues — watching out for clippy warnings around type casting and truncation, making sure there aren't any undocumented panics, and checking for module name repetitions that might need suppression attributes. I should also verify all imports are actually being used and consider any edition 2024 features that might apply.

Looking at the `preprocess_content_blocks` function more closely, I'm using a pattern match on `blocks.get(i + 1)` to check for `ContentBlock::ToolResult`, which is a nested pattern match rather than a let chain, so that should work fine with the current edition.

Let chains are the `&&` syntax combining multiple conditions, which the codebase already uses elsewhere. Now I'm thinking through potential clippy issues, particularly the cast of `state.scroll_offset as u16` in the render function—for very long transcripts this could overflow, so I should use a safer conversion method instead.

Looking at ratatui's API, `Paragraph::scroll` takes a tuple of `u16` values, so I'll clamp the scroll offset to `u16::MAX` before casting to avoid truncation issues. I'm also checking edge cases like when the entries list is empty.

For Progress entries in the transcript, I'll display them as dimmed text since they're not critical to the main display. I need to make sure the `tool_line_map` correctly handles blank separator lines as `None`, and when highlighting the sidebar, I'm checking if the current block index matches the entry's block index.

Since thinking and tool blocks each have their own sequential indices, a thinking block's index will never collide with a tool block's index, so the sidebar highlighting will correctly show nothing when viewing a thinking block. Now I'm ready to put together the complete file.

I need to verify the imports are correct—specifically whether `TranscriptMessage` needs to be imported. Since I'm matching on `TranscriptEntry::Message(msg)` and then passing `msg` to a helper function that explicitly types it as `&TranscriptMessage`, I do need that import.

The task specifies importing `TranscriptEntry, TranscriptMessage, ContentBlock, MessageRole` from `mementor_lib::model`, so I'll go ahead and include all of them. Now I need to check if `jiff` requires an import for the timestamp formatting. Checking the lifetime conversions for `Span::styled` — when I pass a `String` from `format!()`, it converts to `Cow<'static, str>`, giving me a `Span<'static>` which is exactly what I need for my `Vec<Line<'static>>`.

Now I'm verifying that `Paragraph::new()` accepts `Vec<Line<'static>>` in ratatui. I'm verifying the lifetime handling in `highlight_search_in_line` — the function takes and returns `Line<'static>`, and since I'm using `.to_owned()` and `.to_string()` to create owned Strings for the spans, everything stays `'static`. I should also check that `Span::raw` and `Line::from` work correctly with the owned data, and then I can write the file. One more thing to confirm: `TranscriptMessage` is properly exported from `mementor_lib::model`. can import it.

Actually wait, let me re-check the model module:

```rust
pub use transcript::{
    ContentBlock, ConversationSegment, MessageRole, TranscriptEntry, TranscriptMessage,
};
```

Yes, all types are re-exported.

One more concern: I'm using `input.to_string()` in `preprocess_content_blocks` where `input` is `&serde_json::Value`. The `Display` trait for `serde_json::Value` is implemented in the serde_json crate. Since mementor-lib depends on serde_json and I'm calling a method on a type from that crate, the trait impl is available. The method `.to_string()` calls `Display::fmt()`. This works because the trait impl travels with the type, not the crate. Good.

When I destructure `ContentBlock::ToolUse { name, input }` from a reference, I get `name` as `&String` and `input` as `&serde_json::Value`. I can call `to_string()` on the input to get a `String`, and `as_object()` returns an optional reference to the underlying map that I can iterate over.

For the expanded view, I want to format the JSON with line breaks instead of compact output. I'll handle this inline in the match arm rather than extracting to a separate function, since I can't easily name `serde_json::Value` in a public signature without it being a direct dependency. I see the issue now—the preprocessing step already converts the input to a string, so by the time I'm rendering, I've lost the structured data needed to format it as key-value pairs. I should modify the `ProcessedBlock` enum to store both a compact single-line version and an expanded multi-line version of the arguments, then compute the expanded format during preprocessing when I still have access to the original structured input.

Updating the preprocessing logic... Writing JSON formatter... I'm realizing this approach is getting too complicated and fragile. Let me step back and try something simpler instead—just display the compact JSON as-is for the collapsed view, and for the expanded view, I'll extract and show the key-value pairs directly from the input object during preprocessing rather than trying to format the JSON string itself. Finishing the tool block structure with the expanded args lines and result fields, then realizing I need to rethink how the preprocessing handles pairing tool uses with their results since the current approach of checking the next block inside the match arm creates issues with the borrow checker and iteration logic.

Now I'm working through what happens when multiple tool uses appear in sequence followed by their results—the pairing logic needs to handle cases where a ToolUse isn't immediately followed by a ToolResult, so I can't just peek at the next block and skip it.

But actually, looking at how Claude Code structures transcripts, all the content blocks come from a single assistant message, and tool use and result pairs are always sequential in the pattern [ToolUse, ToolResult, ToolUse, ToolResult, ...] interleaved with Text and Thinking blocks, so I shouldn't encounter orphaned results or unpaired tool uses.

My pairing logic of checking if the next block is a ToolResult should handle the common case fine, and if something doesn't pair up, treating it as orphaned text is acceptable.

Now I need to actually write out the complete file instead of continuing to deliberate. Though I should double-check the u16 cast for scroll_offset—if it exceeds u16::MAX it'll truncate, so I might need to add a clippy allow attribute or handle that differently.

Handling the overflow gracefully with `u16::try_from(state.scroll_offset).unwrap_or(u16::MAX)` is better than just casting and ignoring the warning. The other casts like width and height from u16 to usize are safe in both directions, and the saturating subtraction for border_width is fine. I should also make sure the function handles an empty transcript without crashing.

If there are no entries, the lines vector stays empty and the sidebar renders nothing, which is fine. For very small terminals, the layout constraints should prevent issues — even if area.width is 0, the content_width calculation just becomes 0, and the truncation logic handles that gracefully without panicking. Now I need to write out the actual implementation, making sure the block_index stays consistent in the render_message function.

The block_index assignment is deterministic since I iterate entries in the same order each time, so it won't shift unless the entries themselves change. Searching only highlights lines without changing the count or tool_line_map, so that's safe. Expanding a tool does add lines and modify the tool_line_map, which is expected and handled by rebuilding everything on each render. The tricky part is that scroll_offset might jump when a tool above the current view is expanded or collapsed — if I'm viewing line 100 and expand a tool at line 50 that adds 20 lines, the content shifts.

This is a known limitation of scroll-based views, so I'll accept it for now and let users reorient with search or keyboard shortcuts. Now I need to finalize the args_preview handling: in collapsed mode I'll show one truncated line of compact JSON, and in expanded mode I'll display the full args, potentially formatted as key-value pairs for better readability if it's an object.

Actually, I'm going to keep this straightforward—args are always shown as a string, just truncated when collapsed and full when expanded. The key difference is that the result only appears in expanded mode.

The task list is empty from my perspective. This might be because the team lead has the task list and I'm not seeing it. Let me just proceed with the implementation. I've already read all the relevant files.

Let me now create the transcript.rs file. I need to be careful about the output token limit, so let me write the file.

Let me plan the structure:

1. `TranscriptViewState` struct
2. `TranscriptAction` enum
3. `render()` function
4. `handle_key()` function
5. Helper rendering functions

Key design decisions:
- The render function takes `&[TranscriptEntry]` 


- Two-panel layout with the transcript taking up 75% and a tool sidebar on the right at 25%
- Different message types get distinct styling: user messages in green, assistant in blue, thinking blocks in dim gray and collapsible, tool use in bordered boxes, and tool results displayed underneath
- Search functionality triggered with `/` that lets me cycle through results with `n` and `N` with yellow highlighting
- A tool index sidebar showing available tools

Now I'm setting up the Rust module with the necessary imports and starting to build out the core structures.

Defining the state struct to track scroll position, which tools are expanded, sidebar visibility, search state including the query and current match index, and a buffer for typing searches, plus a cached line count from the last render.

Now I'm thinking through the rendering strategy—I could either flatten all entries into individual lines upfront and apply scrolling to that, or build display blocks that group related content together. The block-based approach seems cleaner since I can track which blocks correspond to tool uses for the expand/collapse feature, then render only the visible ones based on the scroll offset. The sidebar would show tool names mapped to their block indices.

For the `expanded_tools` HashSet, I'm realizing it should track indices within a flat list of all tool use blocks across the entire transcript, so each tool call gets a unique number regardless of which entry it came from.

The sidebar needs to display these tools with their corresponding indices, and when the user presses Enter, I need to figure out which tool is at the current scroll position. This means I need to track which lines in the rendered output correspond to which tool uses.

I'm thinking through the architecture: `build_lines()` should take the transcript entries and state, then return the rendered lines, a list of tool information for the sidebar, and a way to map line ranges back to tool indices so the Enter key handler knows what to toggle.

Defining the ToolInfo struct...

Implementing the Rust code... Implementing the Default trait for TranscriptViewState to initialize all fields with their zero values, then defining the TranscriptAction enum for state transitions, and starting to build the render function that will process transcript entries into displayable lines.

Now I'm extracting the lines and tool indices from the entries, updating search matches if a query is active, and calculating the layout split between the transcript view and sidebar based on visibility state.

Handling the search input area... Now I'm handling the search bar rendering—formatting the search text to show either the current input, the active query with match counts, or nothing if there's no search active, then styling it in yellow and placing it in the designated area. After that, I need to handle the sidebar rendering.

Moving into the key handling logic, I'm setting up the main input handler that processes keyboard events and returns actions based on what the user presses. If search input mode is active, I delegate to a specialized search handler, otherwise I check for navigation keys like j/k or arrow keys to scroll through the content.

Handling key input... Writing keyboard handlers... Building the transcript lines... Writing the message builder function... I'm realizing thinking blocks need their own handling separate from tool uses. They should be collapsed by default showing just the first line with ellipsis, and I can make them expandable through the same Enter key mechanism that toggles tool calls.

Writing the preview logic... Continuing with the tool display formatting...

For the collapsed view, I'm creating a single-line preview that shows the tool index, name in bold yellow, and a truncated JSON preview of the arguments in dark gray. Then I'm handling the next content block type for tool results.

Now I'm working through how to display tool results—they should only appear when their parent tool is expanded, so I'm tracking which tool owns each result by using the tool counter to figure out the parent index, then checking if that tool is in the expanded set. If it is, I'm rendering the result header and iterating through the content lines.

Finishing up the rendering logic... Still writing the highlighting logic... Finishing the span reconstruction and then moving into the sidebar rendering function, where I'm styling tool items based on whether they're expanded or not.

Now I'm building the toggle logic to find which tool corresponds to the current scroll position. Handling search input events... Finishing the navigation logic for search matches, then checking whether `serde_json` is available as a dependency—it's not directly in the mementor-tui Cargo.toml, though mementor-lib uses it, so I'd need to add it or work with the existing imports. I'm realizing that relying on transitive dependencies is risky, so I'll stick with what's directly available. The `{:#}` alternate format specifier won't give me pretty-printing for `serde_json::Value` anyway—only the compact form. For the expanded view, I can manually split the JSON string on commas or just truncate it, keeping things simple without adding extra dependencies. I can use the `to_string()` method on the `input` value directly without needing to import `serde_json` explicitly, since the type is already known from the pattern match. Let me just call `input.to_string()` and manually wrap the JSON output for display.

Now I need to handle the line indices from `search_matches` carefully when highlighting the visible portions. I'm realizing that `to_lowercase()` can change string lengths for certain Unicode characters, which means the byte indices from the lowercased text might not align with the original. For a code viewer in a TUI, ASCII-only search is practical enough, so I'll implement a simpler case-insensitive approach that handles the common case reliably.

Writing the search function... I'm verifying that `String` can collect from an iterator of `&str` references using the `FromIterator` trait, which it does implement. Now I need to finalize the implementation while being mindful of Rust edition compatibility and clippy warnings—particularly around the O(n) lookup when checking if a line is in the matches, so I might use a HashSet instead to optimize that.

I'm also catching a potential bug where slicing a string at an arbitrary byte position could panic if it lands in the middle of a multi-byte UTF-8 character, so I'll use `.chars().take()` to safely truncate by character count instead.

Writing the final implementation... I'm verifying the byte length preservation when using `to_ascii_lowercase()` — since it maintains the same byte positions for ASCII characters, slicing the original text with indices from the lowercased version is safe. I'm also confirming that the `matches.contains()` lookup in the highlight function is performant enough for the expected volume of visible lines and matches. Now I need to think through what "highlight current" means for the tool index sidebar — I believe it should highlight whichever tool is closest to the current scroll position — and then I'm ready to write out the full implementation, though I'm also considering edge cases like handling very long transcripts.

The `build_lines` function will process all entries on each render, which could be slow for thousands of messages, but since the existing codebase already uses this pattern for full rerenders at ~10fps, I'll stick with the straightforward approach rather than optimizing prematurely. Now I'm ready to write the file with the planned structure: imports, type definitions, public functions like `render()` and `handle_key()`, and private helpers for building lines, rendering the sidebar, handling search, and managing matches.

Writing the implementation...

Task #1 (fullscreen transcript view) is complete. I've implemented the full `crates/mementor-tui/src/views/transcript.rs` with all requested features:

**Types:**
- `TranscriptViewState` with scroll offset, expanded tools (`HashSet<usize>`), sidebar toggle, search state, plus internal bookkeeping (total_lines, tool_line_map)
- `TranscriptAction` enum: `None`, `Back`, `ScrollTo(usize)`

**Rendering (`render()`):**
- Two-panel layout: left 75% transcript, right 25% tool sidebar (toggleable with `o`)
- Message rendering by type:
  - User: Green `[User]` header with timestamp
  - Assistant: Blue `[Assistant]` header with timestamp
  - Thinking: Dim gray italic, collapsed to first line + "..."
  - ToolUse: Collapsed shows `[idx] ToolName {args_preview...}`, expanded shows bordered box with full JSON args
  - ToolResult: Shown indented under expanded parent tool only (up to 15 lines)
- FileHistorySnapshot, PrLink, Progress entries rendered appropriately
- Scroll position indicator in bottom-right of border
- Empty transcript shows "No transcript data"

**Key bindings (`handle_key()`):**
- `j/k/↑/↓`: Line scroll
- `Ctrl-d/Ctrl-u`: Half-page scroll
- `g/G`: Jump to top/bottom
- `Enter`: Expand/collapse nearest tool (within 5 lines of scroll)
- `/`: Enter search input mode
- `n/N`: Next/prev search match (cycles, scrolls to match)
- `o`: Toggle sidebar
- `Esc`: Clear search first, then Back action

**Search:**
- `/` activates input mode, Enter commits, Esc cancels
- Case-insensitive ASCII search across all rendered lines
- Yellow highlight on current match, dark gray on other matches
- Match counter shown in search bar (`3/15`)

**Tool sidebar:**
- Lists all tool-use blocks with `▶`/`▼` expand indicators
- Highlights nearest tool to scroll position in bold yellow
- Expanded tools shown in yellow

**Quality:**
- `cargo clippy -p mementor-tui -- -D warnings` passes clean
- `cargo fmt --check -p mementor-tui` passes clean
- Used `impl std::fmt::Display` instead of `serde_json::Value` directly to avoid unlinked crate issue
- Used `is_none_or` instead of `map_or(true, ...)` per clippy
- No modifications to any other files (mod.rs already had `pub mod transcript;`)

**Key bindings (`handle_key()`):**
- `j/k/↑/↓`: Line scroll
- `Ctrl-d/Ctrl-u`: Half-page scroll

- `/`: Enter search input mode

**Tool sidebar:**
